records.ety: [OE. destresse, distresse, OF. destresse, destrece, F. dtresse, OF. destrecier to distress, (assumed) LL. districtiare, fr. L. districtus, p. p. of distringere. See Distrain, and cf. Stress.]

defs: Extreme pain or suffering; anguish of body or mind; as, to suffer distress from the gout, or from the loss of friends.
quotes: Not fearing death nor shrinking for distress.

defs: That which occasions suffering; painful situation; misfortune; affliction; misery.
quotes: Affliction's sons are brothers in distress.

defs: A state of danger or necessity; as, a ship in distress, from leaking, loss of spars, want of provisions or water, etc.

The act of distraining; the taking of a personal chattel out of the possession of a wrongdoer, by way of pledge for redress of an injury, or for the performance of a duty, as for nonpayment of rent or taxes, or for injury done by cattle, etc.
The thing taken by distraining; that which is seized to procure satisfaction.
quotes: If he were not paid, he would straight go and take a distress of goods and cattle.
The distress thus taken must be proportioned to the thing distrained for.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Distressed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Distressing.]

defs: To cause pain or anguish to; to pain; to oppress with calamity; to afflict; to harass; to make miserable.
quotes: We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed.

defs: To compel by pain or suffering.
quotes: Men who can neither be distressed nor won into a sacrifice of duty.

defs: To seize for debt; to distrain.
